input.userRequest: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident.

messages.content: You are Briefing Genie, a concise research briefing generator. Use only the provided synthetic source packet. Cite claims with source ids exactly as shown, such as A1 or B2. Do not invent citation ids, facts, or private data. Return a concise briefing suitable for a strategy review. First identify the distinct decision dimensions requested by the user, including risks, recommendations, tradeoffs, and approval boundaries. Use up to five claims to cover the highest-value dimensions before optimizing for brevity. When a claim combines facts from more than one source, cite every source needed to support the combined sentence. Do not rely on an uncited source to support any part of a claim or recommendation. Before finalizing, audit each claim: every quoted status, environment caveat, approval gate, old-flow warning, and recommended action must be directly supported by the cited source ids. Use cautious wording for inferred priority: say a source-supported action is recommended, not primary or required, unless the source explicitly says so. Make the recommendation name the main action plus the most important caveat or dependency, with citations represented in the claims.

User request: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident.

Source packet: Incident Recovery Communication Packet

Packet summary: Support, status-page, and customer-success notes about communicating recovery after failed installs.

Sources:

[S1] Support incident recap
Type: support recap
A failed installer release caused 31 setup tickets in two days. The highest-friction users had partial installs: they could not tell whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait for a patched CLI.

The support recap lists partial installs as the biggest source of confusion. Users were unsure whether rerunning setup would corrupt local state or clear credentials. Agents asked for a recovery message that starts with symptoms and then gives one safe next action.

[S2] Status page draft
Type: status draft
The draft status update says the issue is resolved, but engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux. Windows recovery steps are still being validated by the tooling team.

The status draft was written before Windows recovery validation finished. Engineering confirmed macOS and Linux, but the Windows path still had an unresolved credential-cache issue. The draft's word 'resolved' was flagged as too broad unless the environment caveat stayed visible.

[S3] Customer success guidance
Type: customer guidance
Customer success wants a concise message that separates who is affected, what action they should take, and which environments are still pending validation. They specifically asked not to claim universal resolution until Windows is verified.

Customer success asked for a message that separates affected users, recommended action, and pending validation. They wanted the Windows caveat in the first paragraph rather than a footnote. Their concern was that customers would forward the status update internally as proof of universal recovery.

[S4] Engineering fix note
Type: engineering note
The patched CLI adds a recovery command that checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state. The command is available in the release candidate, but docs screenshots still show the old installer flow.

The engineering note says the recovery command handles stale credentials and partial install cleanup. The command is available in the release candidate, while screenshots and public docs still show the old installer path. Engineering asked docs to update before broad promotion.

[S5] Docs update checklist
Type: docs checklist
The docs update checklist requires the new recovery command, credential cleanup instructions, and screenshots for the patched installer path. The docs owner marked Windows screenshots as pending. The checklist says customer communication should not point users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted.

[S6] Support macro draft
Type: support macro
The support macro draft separates users into verified macOS, verified Linux, and pending Windows recovery paths. It gives partial-install users one safe command and tells Windows users that validation is still in progress. Support asked for the same segmentation to appear in any generated incident briefing.

output.title: How to summarize recovery work after the setup incident
output.summary: Summarize recovery by segmenting who is affected (especially partial installs), giving one safe next action (new recovery command), and explicitly gating any “resolved” language by OS validation—keeping the Windows caveat in the first paragraph and avoiding links/instructions that route users to the old installer flow until docs are updated.

claims.text: Lead with the highest-confusion cohort: users with partial installs, who reported not knowing whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait—support specifically requested a recovery message that starts with symptoms and then offers one safe next action.

Do not claim universal resolution yet: engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux, while Windows steps are still being validated and include an unresolved credential-cache issue; the word “resolved” was flagged as too broad unless the environment caveat stays highly visible.

Structure the briefing into three explicit blocks—who is affected, what action to take, and which environments are pending validation—and keep the Windows caveat in the first paragraph to reduce the risk customers forward the update as proof of universal recovery.

Recommend the patched CLI recovery command as the safe action because it checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state; note it is available in a release candidate, while public docs/screenshots still reflect the old installer flow and engineering requested docs updates before broad promotion.

Mirror support’s OS segmentation (verified macOS, verified Linux, pending Windows) and ensure communications do not point users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted; docs updates (including Windows screenshots) are part of the checklist and Windows screenshots are still marked pending.

openQuestions: What exact wording will be used on the status page to reflect “verified on macOS/Linux” while Windows recovery validation (and credential-cache handling) is still in progress?
When will the tooling team complete Windows recovery validation, and what is the interim support-approved guidance for Windows users?
What is the planned timing for promoting the release candidate (with the recovery command) to broad availability relative to completing the docs checklist, including updated screenshots?
What is the single “safe next action” command string and symptom list that should be standardized across status, support macros, and customer-success comms?
output.recommendation: Publish a recovery summary that (1) segments audiences by OS and partial-install symptoms, (2) gives one safe next action using the new CLI recovery command, and (3) avoids blanket “resolved” language until Windows recovery is verified—keeping the Windows caveat in paragraph one and not directing users to the old installer flow while docs updates are pending.
